title: The Showdown Effect: A Double-Blind Epistemic Isolation (DBEI) Framework for Assessing Generative AI's Reverse-Engineering Capability Boundary
author: Hsu, Chin-Yu
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: This preprint proposes the Double-Blind Epistemic Isolation (DBEI) framework to assess whether generative AI can reconstruct the core architecture of an unpublished, multi-domain complex engineering system using only publicly available prior art. Under epistemic isolation, the AI consistently failed to cross the nonlinear coupling boundaries of the target system; upon disclosure of the complete information at the "showdown trigger," its analytical focus shifted from challenging physical feasibility to scrutinizing procedural and disclosure formalities. All experimental transcripts are sealed with SHA-256 and a Merkle Tree (MerkleRoot independently reproducible), ensuring tamper-evidence. This is a single-target, single-rater proof of concept; broader claims await multi-target, multi-model, and multi-rater validation.
steps_raw: This preprint proposes the Double-Blind Epistemic Isolation (DBEI) framework to assess whether generative AI can reconstruct the core architecture of an unpublished, multi-domain complex engineering system using only publicly available prior art. Under epistemic isolation, the AI consistently failed to cross the nonlinear coupling boundaries of the target system; upon disclosure of the complete information at the "showdown trigger," its analytical focus shifted from challenging physical feasibility to scrutinizing procedural and disclosure formalities. All experimental transcripts are sealed with SHA-256 and a Merkle Tree (MerkleRoot independently reproducible), ensuring tamper-evidence. This is a single-target, single-rater proof of concept; broader claims await multi-target, multi-model, and multi-rater validation.
